title: Vegetation Management Work Order Tracking
spl:
index=oms sourcetype="oms:outage"
| where work_type="vegetation" OR match(lower(outage_cause),"veg|tree|limb")
| eval completed=if(isnotnull(completed_date) AND completed_date!="",1,0)
| stats count as related_events, sum(completed) as completed_orders, dc(feeder_id) as feeders_affected by work_order_id
| eval completion_rate=if(related_events>0, 100*completed_orders/related_events, 0)
| where completed_orders < related_events
| table work_order_id, related_events, completed_orders, feeders_affected, completion_rate
description: Unexecuted clearance work correlates with repeat outages; tying work orders to feeder outage history prioritizes trims and documents regulatory readiness.
value: Service and risk leaders use signals from Vegetation Management Work Order Tracking to move crews, budget, and customer or regulator communications while the situation is still controllable, so the organization steers from live data rather than a post-incident report.
implementation: Ingest vegetation work orders and outage records with shared feeder and span identifiers via HEC from the enterprise work system. Use lookups for span-to-feeder if needed. Monthly reporting for regulatory vegetation cycles; optional join with `fleet:gps` for crew verification. **Domain context:** Many jurisdictions require traceable vegetation clearance cycles near conductors; repeat vegetation-caused outages on uncleared spans are regulatory and wildfire risk signals (where applicable). **Splunk:** `match(lower(outage_cause),"veg|tree|limb")` complements structured `work_type`; tune regex for your OMS vocabulary.
visualization: Table (open vegetation orders), Bar chart (outages vs completed trims by feeder), Timeline (work order lifecycle), Map (feeder segments).
knownFalsePositives: Backlog or cycle-time noise during season trimming surges, contractor mobilization, easement disputes, or new feeder construction that the vegetation program lead tracks separately.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom HEC (GIS / work management).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=oms` `sourcetype="oms:outage"` (feeder_id, outage_cause, work_order_id, work_type, completed_date).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest vegetation work orders and outage records with shared feeder and span identifiers via HEC from the enterprise work system. Use lookups for span-to-feeder if needed. Monthly reporting for regulatory vegetation cycles; optional join with `fleet:gps` for crew verification. **Domain context:** Many jurisdictions require traceable vegetation clearance cycles near conductors; repeat vegetation-caused outages on uncleared spans are regulatory and wildfire risk signals (where applicable). **Splun…

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=oms sourcetype="oms:outage"
| where work_type="vegetation" OR match(lower(outage_cause),"veg|tree|limb")
| eval completed=if(isnotnull(completed_date) AND completed_date!="",1,0)
| stats count as related_events, sum(completed) as completed_orders, dc(feeder_id) as feeders_affected by work_order_id
| eval completion_rate=if(related_events>0, 100*completed_orders/related_events, 0)
| where completed_orders < related_events
| table work_order_id, related_events, completed_orders, feeders_affected, completion_rate
```

Understanding this SPL

**Vegetation Management Work Order Tracking** — Unexecuted clearance work correlates with repeat outages; tying work orders to feeder outage history prioritizes trims and documents regulatory readiness.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=oms` `sourcetype="oms:outage"` (feeder_id, outage_cause, work_order_id, work_type, completed_date). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom HEC (GIS / work management). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: oms; **sourcetype**: oms:outage. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=oms, sourcetype="oms:outage". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where work_type="vegetation" OR match(lower(outage_cause),"veg|tree|limb")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **completed** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by work_order_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **completion_rate** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where completed_orders < related_events` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Vegetation Management Work Order Tracking**): table work_order_id, related_events, completed_orders, feeders_affected, completion_rate


Step 3 — Validate
Compare alarm rates, SCADA values, or meter gaps with the EMS or SCADA operator display and, for AMI, the meter head-end system for the same substation, feeder, device, or trading book and time window. Spot-check a few raw events so field names match operations’ screens.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (open vegetation orders), Bar chart (outages vs completed trims by feeder), Timeline (work order lifecycle), Map (feeder segments).
grandmaExplanation: We watch vegetation work near power lines. We help you see backlog before grow-in threatens reliability.
